texts.orig: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide is the greenhouse gas contribution most to the global warming caused by human activities. In the last years, more and more publications appear as an effort to find a way to reduce the emissions from the largest pollution sources, i.e. power production, steel making and cement production. The research is ongoing on different capture technologies but presently the most promising one is chemical absorption with amine-based aqueous solvents. However, the capital and operating costs are high in the large scale implementation due to the solvent and energy costs. Many of the amines can be harmful to the environment, and some of its degradation products can be even more toxic (Eide-Hagumo et al., 2009). texts.label: text
texts.orig: Postcombustion CO2 capture into aqueous solutions of 3-methylaminopropylamine (N-methyl-1,3propane diamine - MAPA) activated dimethyl-monoethanolamine (2-dimethylaminoethanol - DMMEA) is reported from a laboratory scale pilot plant. The chemicals were selected among others due to their environmental impact and properties for post combustion CO2 capture. The objective of running the pilot plant tests was to obtain data for cyclic capacity, mass transfer rate, specific heat requirements and operational behaviour of the mentioned system. The data are considered used in modeling. The obtained data are compared to other systems, including the 30 mass% (5 M) monoethanolamine (MEA) and potassium sarcosine systems.
texts.text: Postcombustion CO2 capture into aqueous solutions of 3-methylaminopropylamine (N-methyl-1,3propane diamine - MAPA) activated dimethyl-monoethanolamine (2-dimethylaminoethanol - DMMEA) is reported from a laboratory scale pilot plant. The chemicals were selected among others due to their environmental impact and properties for post combustion CO2 capture. The objective of running the pilot plant tests was to obtain data for cyclic capacity, mass transfer rate, specific heat requirements and operational behaviour of the mentioned system. The data are considered used in modeling. The obtained data are compared to other systems, including the 30 mass% (5 M) monoethanolamine (MEA) and potassium sarcosine systems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The overall gas side mass transfer coefficient and heat input were estimated. It was found that the system performs similarly to MEA at lower heat inputs. The reboiler heat load could not be increased to high values due to DMMEA volatility. However, it is expected that the DMMEA/MAPA system will have better performance than MEA if the volatility is managed properly with a better plant design and thus higher reboiler heat duties could be used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Published results stem from different pilot constructions with different design and operational conditions. It may therefore be difficult to compare the results and have a clear understanding of their implications for the various solvent systems. There are several pilot plants constructed in the world, and also a lot of modeling work is done on the topic of solvent system comparison. These models usually show lower energy requirements than the experimental work as heat losses from the plant are normally excluded. Most of the available information coming from the pilot tests is related to 30 mass% (5 M) MEA (Tobiesen et al., 2007, 2008; Mangalapally et al., 2009; Gáspár and Cormos, 2011). The potential of ammonia as a capturing agent could be due to its lower regeneration temperature and energy (80-90 ◦ C), but issues with this system relate to its high volatility and slower kinetics than most amine systems (Rhee et al., 2011). However, at low ammonia concentration the heat requirement can be even higher than for MEA, because the major part of the heat is used for solvent heat-up (Yu et al., 2011). Sterically hindered 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP) pilot data were published by Gabrielsen et al., 2007 from the same pilot plant as used in the present study. From an environmental aspect amino-acid and carbonate systems could have an advantage and several are under development (Aronu et al., 2010; Knuutila et al., 2011; Kumar et al., 2003).

texts.orig: The CESAR EU-project has as a main target to find solvents which have some advantage over the 30 mass% MEA. In this paper a pilot study on the mixed 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system is described. This system was found to have a potential for both lower heat of regeneration and lower environmental impact than MEA (Brúder et al., 2012; Eide-Hagumo et al., 2009).
texts.text: The CESAR EU-project has as a main target to find solvents which have some advantage over the 30 mass% MEA. In this paper a pilot study on the mixed 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system is described. This system was found to have a potential for both lower heat of regeneration and lower environmental impact than MEA (Brúder et al., 2012; Eide-Hagumo et al., 2009).

texts.orig: The absorption/desorption unit is designed to process about 150 Nm 3 /h of gas with a maximum recovery of about 13 kg CO2/h. Detailed information about the absorber and desorber can be found in Table 1. The control system was supplied by Siemens.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During the experimental campaigns several parameters, like gas and liquid flow rates and heat input, were varied. The system is automatically controlled by an online control system, and log files are recorded continuously during operation. CO2 concentrations at the inlet and outlet of the absorber, flow rates of liquid and gas, energy input and temperatures and pressures were logged while running the plant. For each steady state operating point, in addition to logging/registration of parameters, liquid samples were extracted from four locations. A simplified flow sheet for the pilot rig is given in Fig. 2. The liquid enters the top of the absorption column, trickles downwards and reacts with a gas mixture containing CO2 (Yara, 99.999%) and N2 (Yara, 99.99%) which is fed from the bottom of the column. Oxygen was tried to avoid during the test, however most of the degradation would be oxidative in the absorber in real conditions. As the liquid reaches the bottom of the absorber, it is loaded with CO2 and it is sent to the regenerator column via the heat exchanger HEX 1 and pre-heater HEX 4. The liquid flows from top to bottom in the regenerator column and meets the upcoming vapor from an electrically heated reboiler. This vapor contains the energy required to regenerate the liquid and release CO2. The regenerated amine solution from the reboiler outlet flows through the cross flow heat exchanger HEX 1, into a mixing tank, and through the heat exchanger, cooler, HEX 2 to control the solvent temperature to the top of the absorber where the solvent meets the gas flow counter-currently. The pilot is thermally insulated (Rockwool Lamellmatte 541, 6.3 cm absorber, 6.5 cm stripper, k = 0.041 W m -1 K -1 @ 25 ◦ C).

texts.orig: Temperatures were monitored with temperature probes (PT100 resistance temperature sensors) at the inlet and outlet gas/solvent to the absorber, inlet/outlet solvent to stripper, vapor from the reboiler and the solvent in the reboiler. Axial temperature profiles were measured both in the absorber and stripper with PT100 probes. Samples were drawn from chosen locations around the system to obtain liquid composition and solvent density. The places where the liquid samples could be withdrawn are shown in Fig. 2. The liquid sampling points for the measurements (4 sample/run), and the analyses performed can be found in Table 2.

texts.orig: The CO2 contents of the liquid samples were analyzed using an APOLLO 9000 Combustion TOC analyzer. From every set of liquid samples the CO2 concentration of one or two of the samples were determined by the normal wet analysis; precipitation with BaCl2, dissolving BaCO3 with excess HCl and back-titration with NaOH. This was done to ensure the accuracy of the results of the instrumental determination with the Apollo 9000 analyzer. The total alkalinity of the solutions was determined by titration with Metrohm SM Titrino 702 titrator using H2SO4 and standard procedures.

texts.orig: A two-channel CO2 analyzer (Rosemount Binos 100-10 ± 0.1%) connected to the absorber inlet and outlet measured the CO2 content in the gas phase on dry basis. The CO2 analyzers were calibrated once a day using gas mixtures produced from calibrated mass flow controllers (Bronkhorst Hi-Tec -± 0.1% fullscale) for CO2 and N2. At least 4-5 different concentrations were used covering the whole range of interest.

texts.orig: The liquid and gas flow rates, the temperature profiles in the packed columns, the CO2 concentrations in and out of the absorber, the reboiler heat duty, temperatures and pressures in the pipes were continuously logged while running the pilot. Table 3 shows the main data collected during each test run. During a test run, when liquid samples were withdrawn, operational data were simultaneously obtained by averaging over a 15-30 min interval around the sampling time. However, in cases where just a moderate parameter change was made, according to the observations the steady or quasi steady state could be reached sooner. If the operation was ongoing without problems, the pilot was operated around the clock. The range of variation of the main parameters during the tests can be found in Table 4.

texts.orig: the total amine concentration was measured and was quite stable (c ∼ 7 mol NH x /l -Fig. 3). A few times it was necessary to add solution, but always the initial concentration was prepared. However, the total amine concentration can be misleading because the two amine's volatility can be different especially in a CO2 loaded solution. This could also mean that if water would evaporate together with the probably more volatile monoamine DMMEA, the total amine group concentration could remain more or less stable, although there could be a shift in the relative content of the amines. The total amine concentration as function of time can be seen in Fig. 3. Analysis of the individual molar concentrations of both amines to see how the concentrations of the different amines varied over time was done by LC-MS, but only for the initial and final solutions due to limited access to the LC-MS apparatus. The results showed that although the total amine concentration was fairly stable, the DMMEA, as expected, is lost at a higher rate than MAPA (more information in Section 3.5).

texts.orig: In the case of DMMEA/MAPA there are two amines with different properties and it seems that DMMEA makes the solution relatively easy to strip. After CO2 release it becomes more volatile, and as an initial estimate, the molar concentration of DMMEA in the stripper gas is around 11% of that of water. The heat of vaporization is 43.4 kJ/mol which is comparable to water (40.86 kJ/mol).

texts.orig: The sensitivity of the IR detectors, with range 0-10% CO2, is 0.1% on the detector screen. A difference of ± 0.1% in the detector can result in ± 5% in the mass balance, because the difference between the CO2 inlet and outlet was maximum 3.1% in this campaign. This is caused by the combination of a relatively slow absorbent system and a short absorber packing. An estimate of the uncertainty in the detector is ∼± 3%. From this it can be concluded that the uncertainty in the mass transferred based on the gas side should be maximum ∼ ± 10%. This is the biggest possible uncertainty coming from the two channels of the IR detector (CO2 input and output) within one measurement.

texts.text: 3.3. Energy requirement of the DMMEA/MAPA 3/2 system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One of the objectives of this campaign was to find the energy requirement of the DMMEA/MAPA system relative to potassium sarcosine (KSAR) (Knuutila et al., 2011) and 30 mass% MEA (Tobiesen et al., 2007) tested in the lab pilot earlier. The energy input data compared to the reference systems are given in Fig. 6.
texts.text: One of the objectives of this campaign was to find the energy requirement of the DMMEA/MAPA system relative to potassium sarcosine (KSAR) (Knuutila et al., 2011) and 30 mass% MEA (Tobiesen et al., 2007) tested in the lab pilot earlier. The energy input data compared to the reference systems are given in Fig. 6.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From the figure is clear that the highest energy requirements were found for the KSAR system (Knuutila et al., 2011). However, this system also used the lowest absorbent concentration (3.5 mol/l). According to Fig. 6 the DMMEA/MAPA system has a similar stripping energy requirement as 30 mass% MEA. It should be noted that the absolute specific heat duty values obtained in the used pilot cannot be directly compared with data from larger pilots, see e.g. Knudsen et al. (2009). The reason for this is the short absorber column used. The packing height is only 4.36 m which implies that a very limited difference in loading can be achieved. One therefore has to operate with loadings mimicking various sections of an industrial column. For e.g. it is clearly seen from Fig. 6 for 30 mass% MEA that operating in the higher loading regime lowers the specific heat duty (SHD) and for MEA we see SHD values down to 3.6-3.7 GJ/t CO2 captured at the highest loadings. Thus we need to compare the performance of DMMEA/MAPA with MEA and not analyze the absolute values directly. Also for DMMEA/MAPA the specific heat duty decreases with increasing loading and for the highest loadings obtained, the SHD is lower than for MEA and the lowest value obtained was about 3.8 GJ/tCO2. texts.orig: The rich loadings achieved varied between 0.36 and 0.44 mol CO2/mol amine group (NH x ). We believe that even higher rich loadings and better thermal performance could be reached, but with the limitations in packing height this could not be tested and at the end of the campaign we had to move to lower heat inputs because of evaporation from the open parts of the pilot. Normally the specific heat requirement will decrease with increasing heat input. The temperature of the solution in the open part of the apparatus, the holding tank, was from ∼ 40 to ∼ 60 ◦ C during the campaign and at the higher temperatures evaporation was experienced detected by smell.

texts.orig: Comparing, in Fig. 6, the energy requirement of the reboiler for different runs it is clear that the lower specific energy input is found at high rich loadings and/or high liquid flow rates. This is expected because the stripped CO2 is then coming mostly from the higher loading range where the stripping requires the least heat (region closest to the equilibrium). This also shows that the system is quite fast kinetically, because at higher flow rate and lower contact time in the absorber, one can still maintain the extent of absorption in the region of highest loadings, where the kinetics is slower as it is closer to saturation loading and the viscosity is higher. In industrial operation, one would recommend some technical modifications with a better efficiency heat exchanger and a tall absorber. Thereby probably even higher loadings and lower specific heat inputs could be reached.

texts.orig: Flashing may take place in the rich/lean heat exchanger in the pilot plant or in the riser between the heat exchanger and the stripper entry point as no pressure control or restriction exists between these two points. This was visually observed through an installed transparent glass tube where possible CO2 release before the stripper could be checked. Visually it was clear that flashing occurred in almost all cases, and not continually all the time but in a cyclic fashion. This resulted in pressure variations into the stripper. The stripper feed pump was also working in a cyclic manner and the flashing would be particularly strong when the pump provided fresh, rich solution into the vertical 4-5 m tall pipe. If intensive flashing occurs, significant amounts of CO2 can be stripped before the stripper and the entering rich loading is lower than the outlet from the absorber.

texts.orig: The equilibrium pressures ( P bulk ) of MEA and DMMEA/MAPA were calculated from the equilibrium models presented earlier (Brúder et al., 2012). The values given in Fig. 10 thus represent averages for the whole column. The reference area used is the nominal value for the packing (for Mellapak 250Y it is 250 m 2 /m 3 ) and no attempt to relate this to an effective area was made. This implies that effective area deviations from the nominal one are lumped into the Kg value. In order to compare these K g values to other values based on effective area the ratio of effective to nominal area must be used. For most of the runs a liquid load of 10 m 3 /m 2 h was used and the fractional active area under these conditions would be expected to be 0.8 or higher, see Zakeri (2011). From Fig. 10 it can be seen, that the highest mass transfer coefficients are found for the KSAR system. At low loadings the 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system show mass transfer coefficients higher than for 30 mass% MEA but lower than for KSAR. At high loadings the mass transfer coefficient for the 3 M DMMEA/2 M MAPA system decreases rapidly and fall even below those for the MEA system. This is not unreasonable, since we have a system with one fast amine, MAPA, and one slow tertiary amine, DMMEA. The KSAR system shows systematically higher Kg values than both the MEA and DMMEA/MAPA systems. The KSAR values are in accordance with small scale measurements, see Aronu et al. (2011), and significantly higher than for 30 mass% MEA, see Luo (2012).

texts.orig: The gas/liquid ratio (G/L) of 700 m 3 /m 3 used in the DMMEA/MAPA campaign was the gas/liquid ratio approximately used also for 30 mass% MEA. This gas/liquid ratio depends on the cyclic capacity of the solvent, so for solvents with high cyclic capacity the G/L ratio would be higher. By running the absorber with constant gas/liquid ratio the Kg values of the different solvents would be easier to compare. Since the mass transfer rates of the DMMEA/MAPA system are lower than for MEA at high loadings, which actually was expected, this indicates that it will be more difficult to reach a close approach to equilibrium for the DMMEA/MAPA system. As mentioned, this is reasonable since it is a two component system with one slow amine dominating the rates at the high loading end and the viscosity is also increasing with loading.

texts.orig: During this campaign the water wash section was not in direct operation as no water was circulated. However the gas phase passed through the water wash and to close the system a water lock was used to avoid vapor loss through the water circulation pipes. The water lock solution would accumulate volatile components and analyses were made on this solution to estimate amine losses. With a real water wash operation the recovery efficiency would be at least one order of magnitude higher due to a much higher contact area and wash water volume. texts.orig: From Table 7 it can be seen that the lost DMMEA is 11.3 kg and that we have 0.85 kg 'more' MAPA than in the beginning. It should be emphasized that the numbers are estimates. However, it is clear that MAPA is not lost whereas DMMEA would require very effective water wash to remain in the solvent. This loss is mostly due to evaporation, and to a much lower extent to degradation. The campaign was run with the lowest possible oxygen access so the degradation can be assumed to be mainly thermal. As mentioned, water was not circulated in the washing section. Thus the losses experienced during this campaign are not representative of what would be expected in industrial operation, but are useful for evaluating the relative volatility of DMMEA and MAPA.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. Observations during the campaign

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The DMMEA/MAPA 3:2 system was very easy to run, no operational difficulties were experienced. The variation in the lean solvent temperature to the absorber was from ∼ 40 to 60 ◦ C. Significant smell was noticed especially at higher heat loads and it can be concluded that the smell came from the more volatile DMMEA. Besides the absorption kinetics and the regeneration energy data, the pilot plant campaign gave knowledge about the behaviour of the solvent during the campaign. The pilot is closed but the holding tank is semi-closed and has to be opened from time to time and this is considered the biggest source of evaporation and smell.

texts.orig: Foaming was not observed during the campaign at the used gas flow rate, ∼ 127 m 3 /h. Also no solid precipitate was formed during the campaign. Degradation of the solvent during the campaign was not measured, but all the campaign was conducted with the least possible oxygen contact. An oxidative and thermal degradation study is needed in the next steps.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The energy requirement and the overall mass transfer coefficient of the DMMEA/MAPA 3/2 system was found to be comparable to 30 wt% MEA both with regard to energy demand and kinetics in the operating range tested in these experiments. However, the system is believed to have some energetic advantage over MEA on a larger scale with a tall absorber and operations at higher heat loads and with an effective water wash system. The big advantage of this system compared to MEA and AMP/PZ is the moderate environmental impact. Before other large scale experiments it is proposed to make an oxidative and thermal degradation study of both chemicals DMMEA and MAPA. The minimum energy requirement during the experimental campaign was ∼ 3 . 8 Gt / t CO 2 which could be lowered at higher heat loads and with a taller absorber. The reboiler heat load was in the range 4-9.1 kW and this resulted in reboiler temperatures from 105 to 115 ◦ C. At the highest temperature the stripper pressure was 2.18 bar indicating the high CO2 pressure in the system. The low heat input made it difficult to strip below loadings of 0.28 mol/mol NH x . From the data it can be seen that lower rich loadings and lower flow rates increase the heat requirement. The cyclic capacity was limited to 0.16 mol/mol NH x at the used heat duty.

The reboiler heat load was in the range 4-9.1 kW and this resulted in reboiler temperatures from 105 to 115 ◦ C. At the highest temperature the stripper pressure was 2.18 bar indicating the high CO2 pressure in the system. The low heat input made it difficult to strip below loadings of 0.28 mol/mol NH x . From the data it can be seen that lower rich loadings and lower flow rates increase the heat requirement. The cyclic capacity was limited to 0.16 mol/mol NH x at the used heat duty.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The LC/MS analyses showed that the final system changed somewhat from the initial one by a lowering of DMMEA and an increase in MAPA concentrations. When the MAPA concentration goes up, the system becomes faster at low and intermediate loadings. However, at high loadings an opposite effect could appear due to the increased viscosity. For the desorber, as MAPA is faster and more viscous, it would be more difficult to strip at high MAPA concentrations (higher energy needed). As DMMEA is more volatile than MEA, operating the DMMEA/MAPA mixture would require better water wash in order to reduce vaporization losses to acceptable levels.
